sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 1 action in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: LEFT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 23, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 22 of about 28 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Interesting! The LEFT action moved something. Let me check what changed. The N(c) token was at cols 30-32, and now it seems to have moved. But looking at the image, I see the N token with white center is now outside the left container (to the left of it), and the N token with green center is still at cols 30-32.
Wait, that's confusing. Let me check the segmentation to see what actually happened.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Check what changed
seg = current_frame.segmentation['nodes']
print("=== N tokens ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")
        if n['children']:
            child = next(x for x in seg if x['id'] == n['children'][0])
            print(f"    center: color={child['color']} at {child['boundary']}")

# Check containers
print("\n=== Containers ===")
for n in seg:
    if n['color'] == 'c' and n['pixels'] == 16:
        print(f"  boundary={n['boundary']} children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Wait, something weird happened. The LEFT action moved N(W) OUT of the left container! Now:
- N(c) is at cols 30-32 (unchanged)
- N(W) is at cols 9-11, rows 33-35 (moved LEFT from inside the container)
- The left container at cols 11-15 is now EMPTY (no children shown)
So the LEFT action moved N(W) out of the left container and to the left. This means the active token was N(W) inside the container, not N(c) at cols 30-32.
Wait, but I clicked on N(c) inside the container earlier (step 21, MOUSE at row 34, col 13). That should have made N(c) the active token. But the LEFT action moved N(W), not N(c).
Oh wait, I'm confused about the token positions. Let me re-trace:
- Before step 21 (MOUSE): N(c) was inside the left container, N(W) was at cols 30-32
- After step 21 (MOUSE at 34, 13): N(W) is inside the left container, N(c) is at cols 30-32 (swap happened)
- After step 22 (LEFT): N(W) moved LEFT from inside the container to cols 9-11
So the LEFT action moved N(W) which was inside the left container. This means the active token is N(W), not N(c). But I clicked on N(c) inside the container at step 21, which should have made N(c) active.
Wait, actually, at step 21, I clicked on the N token inside the container, which at that time was N(c). After the swap, it became N(W). So the active token is the one I clicked on, which is now N(W) inside the container.
So the active token follows the object, not the color. When I click on an N token, that token becomes active, and its center color can change via swaps, but it remains the active token.
OK so now:
- Active token: N(W) at cols 9-11 (just moved out of left container)
- Other token: N(c) at cols 30-32
- Left container: empty
- Right container: empty (on the other side of the wall)
I need to figure out the goal. Let me think about what configuration would solve the level.
Given that:
1. N tokens can move
2. Clicking swaps center colors
3. N tokens can enter/exit containers
4. The purple wall blocks movement
Maybe the goal is to have:
- N(W) in the left container (white center matches white interior)
- N(B) or N(c) in the right container
But the right container is on the other side of the wall, so N tokens can't reach it.
OR, maybe the goal is:
- N(W) in the right container (white center matches white interior)
- N(B) or N(c) in the left container
But N(W) can't reach the right container due to the wall.
Hmm, let me try a different hypothesis. What if the goal is to have BOTH N tokens in the SAME container? Or what if the goal is to have the N tokens in specific positions (not in containers)?
Actually, let me try to move N(W) back into the left container and see if the level completes. Maybe the goal is just to have N(W) in the left container.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# N(W) is at cols 9-11, left container is at cols 11-15
# Move RIGHT to get back into the container
action(['RIGHT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
